annotations.label: Mathematical notation

anchor.snippet: comprises the symbols used to write mathematical equations and formulas

annotations.note: A meta-description of mathematical notation itself, not a mathematical object Mathlib formalizes.

annotations.label: Rhetorical stage

anchor.snippet: where calculations are performed by words and tallies, and no symbols are used

annotations.note: A historiographical notion about the development of notation, with no Mathlib counterpart.

annotations.label: Syncopated stage

anchor.snippet: where frequently used operations and quantities are represented by symbolic syntactical abbreviations

annotations.note: A historiographical stage of notation, not a formal mathematical statement.

annotations.label: Symbolic stage

anchor.snippet: where comprehensive systems of notation supersede rhetoric

annotations.note: A historiographical stage of notation, not a formal mathematical statement.

annotations.label: Tally marks

anchor.snippet: numbers expressed as tally marks , with each tally representing a single unit

annotations.note: Tally-mark numeral representation is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: YBC 7289 approximation of √2

anchor.snippet: that is accurate to an equivalent of six decimal places

annotations.note: Mathlib has Real.sqrt 2 but not this specific historical sexagesimal approximation.

annotations.label: Babylonian sexagesimal numeral system

anchor.snippet: Babylonian mathematics were written using a sexagesimal

annotations.note: The base-60 numeral system as such is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Finite expansions in base 60

anchor.snippet: has a finite expansion in base 60

annotations.note: No theorem characterizing which rationals have terminating base-60 expansions was found in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Finite decimal expansions

anchor.snippet: only reciprocals of multiples of 2 and 5 have finite decimal expansions

annotations.note: No theorem characterizing terminating decimal expansions of reciprocals was found in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Egyptian hieroglyphic numeration

anchor.snippet: Egyptian mathematics had symbols for one, ten, one hundred, one thousand

annotations.note: Egyptian hieroglyphic numeration is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Rhetorical form of an equation

anchor.snippet: The thing plus one equals two

annotations.note: An illustrative rhetorical phrasing of an equation, not a formal statement to formalize.

annotations.label: Pythagorean theorem

anchor.snippet: in a right-angled triangle the area of the square on the hypotenuse

annotations.note: Verified Mathlib states the Pythagorean theorem in inner-product-space norm form (and angle-at-point form), generalizing the planar right triangle.

annotations.label: Abstract or pure mathematics

anchor.snippet: deals with concepts like magnitude and quantity without regard to any practical application

annotations.note: A philosophical description of pure mathematics, not a formalizable object.

annotations.label: Archimedes' area under a parabola

anchor.snippet: to calculate the area under the arc of a parabola with the summation of an infinite series

annotations.note: Although integration and geometric series exist in Mathlib, Archimedes' quadrature of the parabola is not formalized as such.

annotations.label: Euclid's lemma

anchor.snippet: captures a fundamental property of prime numbers

annotations.note: Verified Nat.Prime.dvd_mul states p ∣ m*n ↔ p ∣ m ∨ p ∣ n, exactly Euclid's lemma.

annotations.label: Attic (acrophonic) numeration

anchor.snippet: since it was based on the first sound of the numeral

annotations.note: Greek Attic/acrophonic numeration is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Milesian (Ionian) numeration

anchor.snippet: constructed by partitioning the twenty-four letters of the Greek alphabet

annotations.note: Greek Milesian/Ionian numeration is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Writing 88,888,888 in Milesian numerals

anchor.snippet: the number 88,888,888 would be written as

annotations.note: A specific Milesian-numeral rendering, with no Mathlib counterpart.

annotations.label: Diophantus' notation for powers

anchor.snippet: an unknown number was called s

annotations.note: Diophantine syncopated notation for powers is a historical notation, not formalized.

annotations.label: Huama numeral system

anchor.snippet: this huama numeral system is only used for displaying prices

annotations.note: The Chinese huama (Suzhou) numeral system is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Brahmagupta's syncopated notation

anchor.section: Indian and Arabic numerals and notation
anchor.snippet: Addition was indicated by placing the numbers side by side

annotations.note: Brahmagupta's historical syncopated notation conventions are not formalized.

annotations.label: Fibonacci sequence (rabbit problem)

anchor.section: Indian and Arabic numerals and notation
anchor.snippet: the growth of a rabbit population ends up being the Fibonacci sequence

annotations.note: Verified Nat.fib defines the Fibonacci sequence; the rabbit problem is its motivating example.

annotations.label: Newton's derivative notation

anchor.snippet: Newton's notation was simply a dot or dash placed above the function

annotations.note: Mathlib formalizes the derivative (deriv) but not Newton's dot notation as such.

annotations.label: Leibniz's derivative notation

anchor.snippet: used the letter d as a prefix to indicate differentiation

annotations.note: Mathlib formalizes differentiation but not Leibniz's dy/dx notational convention itself.

annotations.label: Integral symbol

anchor.snippet: the integral symbol is an elongated S

annotations.note: The historical origin of the ∫ symbol is a notation fact, not a formalizable statement.

annotations.label: Functional notation

anchor.snippet: a letter, as a symbol of operation, is combined with another which is regarded as a symbol of quantity

annotations.note: A description of operator/functional notation, not a formal mathematical object.

annotations.label: Inverse operations and functions

anchor.snippet: are symbols of inverse operations

annotations.note: Mathlib formalizes inverse functions (Function.invFun) but not the historical symbols for inverse operations described here.

annotations.label: Cramer's Rule

anchor.snippet: developed Cramer's Rule for solving linear systems

annotations.note: Verified Matrix.cramer with Matrix.mulVec_cramer formalizes Cramer's rule for linear systems.

annotations.label: Capital-sigma summation notation

anchor.snippet: an enlarged form of the upright capital Greek letter sigma

annotations.note: Mathlib has Finset.sum (∑) but the history/symbol of sigma notation is not a formalizable statement.

annotations.label: Function notation f(x)

anchor.snippet: to represent a function of

annotations.note: The f(x) notational convention is a notation fact, not a formalizable mathematical statement.

annotations.label: Proportionality

anchor.snippet: Proportionality is the ratio of one quantity to another

annotations.note: No dedicated proportionality definition was found in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Field (Dedekind)

anchor.snippet: defined a field to be a set of real or complex numbers which is closed under the four arithmetic operations

annotations.note: Verified the Field class formalizes fields abstractly, generalizing Dedekind's subfields of ℝ/ℂ.

annotations.label: Aleph numbers

anchor.snippet: with natural number subscripts to denote cardinality in infinite sets

annotations.note: Verified Cardinal.aleph defines the aleph cardinals indexing infinite cardinalities.

annotations.label: Einstein notation

anchor.snippet: which summed over a set of indexed terms in a formula

annotations.note: The Einstein summation convention is a notational shorthand not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Mathematical abstraction

anchor.snippet: a process of extracting the underlying essence of a mathematical concept

annotations.note: A philosophical description of abstraction, not a formalizable object.

annotations.label: Boolean algebra symbols

anchor.snippet: Symbols found in Boolean algebra include

annotations.note: Mathlib formalizes the BooleanAlgebra structure, though the statement concerns its symbols specifically.

annotations.label: Boolean logical statement

anchor.snippet: one can make logical statements such as

annotations.note: An illustrative Boolean expression, not a theorem or definition to formalize.

annotations.label: Propositional calculus

anchor.snippet: Most of these symbols can be found in propositional calculus

annotations.note: Mathlib does not formalize propositional calculus as a separate deductive system (it uses Lean's native Prop).

annotations.label: Predicate logic

anchor.snippet: expands on propositional logic by the introduction of variables

annotations.note: Mathlib's model theory formalizes first-order (predicate) logic via FirstOrder.Language, though not under this exact framing.

annotations.label: Gödel's incompleteness theorem

anchor.snippet: there correspond recursive class signs

annotations.note: Mathlib proves Gödel's Beta Function Lemma as a step toward the First Incompleteness Theorem, but the incompleteness theorem itself is not formalized.

annotations.label: Gödel numbering of a statement

anchor.snippet: There exists a number x such that it is not y

annotations.note: Mathlib provides encodings of first-order terms/formulas, related to Gödel numbering, but not the specific arithmetization of this statement.

annotations.label: Klein–Gordon equation

anchor.snippet: proposed the Klein–Gordon equation to describe relativistic particles

annotations.note: The Klein–Gordon equation is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Dirac equation

anchor.snippet: to explain the behavior of the relativistically moving electron

annotations.note: The Dirac equation is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Bra–ket notation

anchor.snippet: is a standard notation for describing quantum states

annotations.note: Mathlib has inner product spaces but does not formalize Dirac bra–ket notation.

annotations.label: d'Alembert operator

anchor.snippet: is the Laplace operator of Minkowski space

annotations.note: The d'Alembertian (Minkowski-space wave operator) is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Levi-Civita symbol

anchor.snippet: also known as the permutation symbol

annotations.note: Mathlib has the permutation sign (Equiv.Perm.sign) underlying the Levi-Civita symbol, but not the indexed symbol itself.

annotations.label: Cycle notation

anchor.snippet: the convention for writing down a permutation in terms of its constituent cycles

annotations.note: Mathlib formalizes decomposition of permutations into disjoint cycles (cycleFactorsFinset, IsCycle), though not the written cycle notation convention.
